questions.question_text: What is the blood group?

During the determination of blood group with monoclonal antibodies (Tsoliclon-test), agglutination was obtained with Tsoliclon Anti-B and no agglutination was registered with Tsoliclon Anti-A. Indicate the correct blood group.
The most frequent way of blood transfusion is:
What temperature is required for RBC (red blood cells) concentrate preservation?
Select the correct method of blood transfusion for biological test?

answers.text: Bolus transfusion of 25 ml of blood, repeated twice with interval of 3-5 minutes
Bolus transfusion of 25 ml of blood, repeated thrice with interval of 3-5 minutes
Slow transfusion of blood during 15 minutes, followed by 3-5 minutes of expectation
Single bolus transfusion of 25 ml of blood
Biological test is performed only in case of plasma transfusion

questions.question_text: Method of blood autotransfusion is performed as follows:

answers.text: Collecting of blood from patient is done several weeks before elected surgery
Several samplings of 400-500 mL of blood with an interval of one week are performed
Several samplings of 100-200 mL of blood with an interval of two days are performed
Last collecting is made no later than 12 hours prior to surgery
Last collecting is made no later than 72 hours prior to surgery

questions.question_text: Acute preoperative normovolemic haemodilution means the following measures:

answers.text: Exfusion of 400-500 mL of patient's blood one week before surgery
Exfusion of 750-1000 mL of patient's blood immediately before surgery
Replacement of collected blood with the blood substitutes solutions
Replacement of collected blood with the donor plasma
The collected blood is transfused back to the patient during or after surgery

questions.question_text: In a patient with traumatic rupture of the spleen and hemoperitoneum, the blood from abdominal cavity was collected using the “Cell Saver” device and transfused intravenously to the patient. How is called this method?
Blood reinfusion can be performed in case of:

answers.text: Traumatic amputation of the lower limb

questions.question_text: Reinfusion of blood collected from the abdominal cavity is impossible in case of:
What complications of blood transfusion do not refer to the infectious?
Specify which of the listed conditions are related to surgical infection.
What infectious processes are related to acute specific surgical infection?
What does suggest the symptom of fluctuations in the surgical soft tissue infection?

answers.text: Formation of the cavity filled with pus
Presence of inflammatory process in the phase of infiltration
Presence of gas in the tissues
Involvement in the pathological process of deeper lying organs

questions.question_text: Specify the typical changes in blood analysis during acute surgical infection.

answers.text: Shift of leukocyte formula to the left

questions.question_text: In case of furuncle a purulent inflammation initially develops in:
In which cases are required hospitalizations of patients with furuncle?

answers.text: With furuncle of hip in a young patient without comorbidities
With furuncle in phase of abscess formation in a patient with severe diabetes
With the localization of furuncle on the foot and inability to support the limb
With the localization of furuncle in the nasolabial triangle

questions.question_text: A purulent inflammation of sweat glands is called:
Which of the statements relating to the phlegmon are correct?

answers.text: Suppurative process spreads along fascial and adipose spaces
An accumulation of pus is limited by pyogenic capsule
Symptoms of intoxication are usually not expressed
Condition of patients always remains satisfactory
Patients require hospitalization and emergency surgery under general or regional anesthesia

questions.question_text: Carbuncle is characterized by the following features:

answers.text: Usually develops in the occipital or interscapular areas
Does not develop in diabetic patients
Skin over the focus of inflammation is not altered
An abscess has the form of "honeycomb"
Symptom of fluctuation is not typical

questions.question_text: A typical causative microbial agent of erysipelas is a:
Specify the clinical forms of erysipelas.
Specify the typical symptoms of acute mastitis.

answers.text: Nipple retraction on the side of inflammation
Pain and bloating in the mammary gland
Skin in the form of "orange peel"
Serous and hemorrhagic discharge from the nipple

questions.question_text: According to classification of surgical infection by clinical evolution, tetanus refers to:
What characterizes lymphangitis?

answers.text: Is an acute bacterial infection of lymphatic vessels
Is a purulent inflammation of lymph nodes
Is a complication of another infectious process

questions.question_text: What differentiates the abscess from the phlegmon?

answers.text: Severe pain in the lesion area
Collection of pus is limited from surrounding tissues by capsula

questions.question_text: On what basis bleeding are classified into "per diabrosis", "per rexin" and "per diapedesis"?

answers.text: In accordance to the anatomical principles
In accordance to the mechanism of occurring
In accordance to the external environment
In accordance to the time of occurring

questions.question_text: Which of these bleeding refers to the internal?

answers.text: Bleeding from the cutting leg wound

questions.question_text: Which of these bleeding refers to the internal intracavitary?

answers.text: Bleeding in to the pleural cavity

questions.question_text: Specify the possible causes of bleeding.

answers.text: Destruction of vessel wall in a purulent process
Disturbances of permeability of the vascular wall

questions.question_text: Specify the consequences of bleeding?
Specify the compensatory reaction of organism to hemorrhage.
Specify the pathological phenomena of organism, which develop in the terminal phase of hemorrhage.
Specify the local symptoms of gastrointestinal bleeding?
A "coffee-grounds" vomiting is characteristic for:
Specify the general symptoms of hemorrhage.
Which laboratory parameters reflect the severity of blood loss?
What methods of hemostasis refer to temporary?

answers.text: Maximal flexion of extremity in a joint

questions.question_text: What definition most appropriately describes sepsis?

answers.text: Presence of bacteria in the bloodstream with purulent metastasis into the tissues and organs
A microbial phenomenon characterized by a local inflammatory response to the presence of microorganisms
Presence and active multiplication of bacteria in the bloodstream
Presence of bacteria in the bloodstream
An infectious process accompanied by an abnormal systemic inflammatory reaction

questions.question_text: What is the septicemia?

answers.text: An infectious process accompanied by an acute inflammatory reaction with systemic manifestations
A microbial phenomenon characterized by a local inflammatory response to the presence of microorganisms
Presence and active multiplication of bacteria in the bloodstream
Presence of bacteria in the bloodstream
Presence of bacteria in the bloodstream with purulent metastasis into the tissues and organs

questions.question_text: For diagnosis of systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) the following criteria are used:
What confirms the presence of systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS)?
Which of the following laboratory results confirms the bacteremia?

answers.text: High contents of procalcitonin in the blood

questions.question_text: The most frequent causative bacterial agent of gas gangrene is:
Specify the wounds with a high risk of anaerobic clostridial infection of soft tissues (gas gangrene) development.

answers.text: With massive damage of muscles and bones
With profound and narrow wound channel (gunshot wound)
With ischemia of tissue caused by arterial injury, or a hemostatic tourniquet application
Lacerated wounds with a large skin defect

questions.question_text: Crepitus defined on palpation in anaerobic clostridial infection of soft tissue is associated with:

answers.text: Penetration of air from the gastrointestinal tract into the wound in postoperative form of infection
Friction of bone fragments in the wounds received in open fractures
Penetration of air from the external environment through the wound channel

questions.question_text: What are the main goals of large longitudinal incisions (fasciotomy) in anaerobic clostridial infection (gas gangrene) of extremity?

answers.text: To simplify the excision of necrotized and visual devitalized tissues
To decrease the compartment syndrome and tissue ischemia
To improve the access of air to wound channel
To prevent the multiplication of clostridia spores

questions.question_text: What is correctly characterizes tetanus?

answers.text: Although rare, the tetanus cannot be eradicated
Infection caused by anaerobic microorganisms with preferential affection of fat and conjunctive tissues
Neonatal tetanus accounts for 50% of the tetanus-related deaths in developing countries
The disease is not passed from one person to the next
Mortality rate for severe tetanus may be as high as 60%

questions.question_text: What form of tetanus is less dangerous?
Specify the measures for tetanus prevention.

answers.text: Administration of pharmacologic agents that treat muscle spasms
Administration of tetanus toxoid in combination with diphtheria toxoid and pertussis vaccine (DTP) to children
Passive immunization with human tetanus immune globulin

questions.question_text: The increased incidence of sepsis is registered among:
Specify the pathogen, which is least likely to cause sepsis.
What pathophysiological mechanisms contribute to the development of ischemia and create anaerobic conditions in anaerobic clostridial infection of soft tissues (gas gangrene)?

answers.text: Spread of gas along the fascial planes
Increased pressure in the fascial spaces due to edema
Disturbance of circulation due to thrombosis of peripheral blood vessels

questions.question_text: Non-clostridial anaerobic infection can clinically manifest as phlegmon with extensive damage of the following tissues:
The penetration of pathogenic organisms in the soft tissues of hands most often occurs:

answers.text: Through the callus, cracks of the skin
With small foreign bodies (splinters, pieces of metal)
From infected phalangeal and wrist bones

questions.question_text: Specify the superficial forms of felon.
The term "pandaktylitis" refers to:

answers.text: Purulent inflammation of all fingers of hand
Purulent inflammation of all digits of foot
Purulent inflammation of all tissues of one finger of hand
Purulent inflammation of all tissues of one digit of foot
Fusion of all fingers of hand or foot

questions.question_text: Edema of the dorsal surface of hand in phlegmon of the palmar surface:

answers.text: Is due exceptionally to rupture of pus on the dorsal surface of hand
Is due to features of lymph drainage
Appears early as a result of friable structure of the subcutaneous tissue

questions.question_text: What location of abscess is defined as a felon?

answers.text: Palmar surface of nail phalanx of hand finger
Plantar surface of nail phalanx of feet finger
Dorsal surface of proximal phalanx of hand finger

questions.question_text: What characterizes the paronychia?

answers.text: On pressing from under nail fold is released a drop of pus
Infection results from trauma to the eponychial or paronychial region
In the nail fold appear mild pain, swelling and redness
Abscess can spread to the palmar surface of nail phalanx in the form of "hourglass"

questions.question_text: What characterizes the bone felon?

answers.text: Bone felon of I and V fingers can spread to the deep cellular spaces of forearm
As a rule, this is an outcome of untreated subcutaneous felon
Infection penetrates in the bone through the cracks of skin
Typical localization is the nail phalanx

questions.question_text: Radiographic changes at bone felon appear:

answers.text: On the next day after the onset of disease
After 3-5 days after the onset of disease
After 5-7 days after the onset of disease
After 10-14 days after the onset of disease
After 1.5-2 months after the onset of disease

questions.question_text: Specify the anatomical forms of phlegmon of the dorsal hand surface.
What are the main causes of malnutrition in surgical patients?

answers.text: Interruption of normal food intake during the preoperative diagnostic tests
A pathological process itself that affects the patient
Administration of antibiotics, which reduce nutrient absorption
Depressed state and loss of appetite in patients, who are awaiting surgery
Restriction of food intake after majority of the surgical procedures

questions.question_text: Which from laboratory parameters is most closely correlates with the body protein deficiency?
The calculation of the body mass index is done using following formula:

answers.text: 48.1 kg for height 152 cm plus 1.1 kg for every 1 cm over 152 cm

questions.question_text: What values of the body mass index are corresponding to morbid obesity?
For what patients the enteral feeding is indicated?

answers.text: Who have functional digestive tract, but unable sustain an adequate oral intake

questions.question_text: For what patients the parenteral nutrition is indicated?

answers.text: In early postoperative period after partial gastrectomy
In early postoperative period after hemorrhoidectomy

questions.question_text: What complications are typical for morbid obesity?
What postoperative complications are characteristics for patients with morbid obesity?
What ways are used for enteral feedings administration?
The local manifestations of wounds are following:
Severity of pain in wound depends on the following factors:

answers.text: Number of nerve endings in the tissues of damaged zone

questions.question_text: Intensity of bleeding from a wound is determined by:

answers.text: Diameter and type (arterial or venous) of damaged vessel

questions.question_text: What wounds are distinguished depending on the nature of traumatic agent?
To what type of wounds according to the nature of traumatic agent should be attributed a surgical incision?
Specify the clinical features of stab wounds.

answers.text: Dehiscence of wound margins is significant
High risk of injury of deep anatomical structures
Dehiscence of wound margins is not significant

questions.question_text: Specify the type of wounds, which is the most contaminated.
According to classification by grade of contamination, wounds are divided into:
It is known that purulent process in the wound develops when the concentration of microorganisms more than:

answers.text: 105 (100.000) of microorganisms on 1 gram of tissue
104 (10.000) of microorganisms on 1 gram of tissue
103 (1.000) of microorganisms on 1 gram of tissue
102 (100) of microorganisms on 1 gram of tissue
101 (10) of microorganisms on 1 gram of tissue

questions.question_text: What wounds of the abdomen should be considered as penetrating?

answers.text: Wounds, associated with injury of skin, subcutaneous adipose tissues, aponeurosis, parietal peritoneum, and bowel
Wounds, associated with injury of skin and subcutaneous adipose tissues
Wounds, associated with injury of skin, subcutaneous adipose tissues, and aponeurosis
Wounds, associated with injury of skin, subcutaneous adipose tissues, aponeurosis, and muscles of the anterior abdominal wall
Wounds, associated with injury of skin, subcutaneous adipose tissues, aponeurosis, muscles of the anterior abdominal wall, and parietal peritoneum

questions.question_text: What zones of tissue alteration are distinguished in gunshot wounds?

answers.text: Zone of molecular concussion and secondary necrosis

questions.question_text: What features differentiate the gunshot wound?

answers.text: Presence of three zones of tissue alteration
Complicated anatomical character of wound channel
Primary surgical processing of wound is finished by placement of primary sutures

questions.question_text: The following phases of wound healing process are distinguished:
What cells possess a main role in the second phase of wound healing process (phase of proliferation)?
What is granulation tissue?

answers.text: Epithelial tissue, which covers wound defect
Necrotic tissue with high contents of microorganisms
Gentle connective tissue with newly formed capillaries
Dense clot in the wound, which is formed as a result of adhesion and aggregation of platelets and thrombosis of capillaries and small veins

questions.question_text: Wound epithelization begins:

answers.text: From the area of wound with the best blood supply
Simultaneously from the entire surface of wound

questions.question_text: Types of wound healing include:
How, as usual, heal superficial wounds?
To local complication of wounds refers:
What complications occur in the third phase of an abdominal wound healing process (phase of epithelization and reorganization of scar)?
What refers to the measures of first medical aid for wounds?

answers.text: Primary surgical processing of the wound
Application of aseptic bandage on the wound

questions.question_text: A primary surgical processing of contaminated cut wound include:
What type of suture is applied after primary surgical processing of the gunshot wound?
For the early lysis and removal of necrotic tissue from the wound is used:
In what circumstances the injury, even small-caliber vessels may be accompanied by severe bleeding, and to be life-threatening for patient?

answers.text: In patients with low blood pressure

questions.question_text: In case of a large wound of the anterior abdomen with evisceration of inner organs, the first medical aid consists in:

answers.text: Clean the skin surrounding the wound with any antiseptic
Immediate suturing of the abdominal wound
Clean the eviscerated inner organs with any antiseptic

questions.question_text: Primary deferred sutures on the wound are placed:

answers.text: During first days after primary surgical processing of the wound, prior to the growth of granulation tissue
Immediately after primary surgical processing of the wound
After development of granulation, but prior to formation of scar tissue
After excision of granulation tissue in the wound
After development of scar tissue and phenomenon of wound contraction

questions.question_text: Deep vein thrombosis can lead to development of:
Select the possible causes for development of acute limb ischemia.

answers.text: Stenosis of the vessel lumen with an atherosclerotic plaque

questions.question_text: Sensorial disorders (paresthesia, hypo- or anesthesia) and motor deficit (paresis, paralysis) are characteristic for:
Specify clinical signs, which are characteristic for the final stage of acute limb ischemia:

answers.text: Severe hip edema and foot hyperemia
Marmoreal aspect of the limb (mottling) and hypothermia of the foot
Foot paralysis and flexion knee contracture

questions.question_text: The group of symptoms known as "6Р" is characteristic for:
Indicate the signs that are not refer to the "6Р" group of symptoms.
Peripheral arterial embolism is manifested with syndrome of:
What comorbidities (concomitant diseases) are typical for patients with acute limb ischemia caused by arterial embolism?
Indicate the typical symptoms of acute ischemia of lower limb.

answers.text: Sensory deficit (lack of sensation) at the level of the foot
Motor deficit (limitation or lack of movements) at the level of the foot
Systolic bruit over the arteries of the foot

questions.question_text: Specify clinical situations which correspond to diabetic foot syndrome definition provided by WHO.

answers.text: Diabetic neuropathy without foot ulcer, gangrene or infection
Infected plantar wound in diabetic patient without neuro- and angiopathy
Foot ulcer associated with diabetic neuropathy
Toe gangrene associated with angiopathy in diabetic patient
Foot ulcer in non-diabetic patient with atherosclerosis

questions.question_text: Foot ulcer develops in:

answers.text: Less than 1% of patients with diabetes mellitus
Less than 5% of patients with diabetes mellitus
More than 50% of patients with diabetes mellitus
More than 90% of patients with diabetes mellitus

questions.question_text: Specify the most frequent form of diabetic foot.
Specify the form of diabetic foot which most frequently results in major amputation
Screening for diabetic foot syndrome includes the following tests:

answers.text: Foot inspection for ulcers, gangrene, infection
Duplex ultrasound of arteries of lower limbs

questions.question_text: For neuropathic form of diabetic foot are characteristic:

answers.text: Presence of painless plantar ulcers with hyperkeratosis
Presence of very painful necrosis at the toes

questions.question_text: For ischemic form of diabetic foot are characteristic:

answers.text: Presence of painless plantar ulcers with hyperkeratosis
Presence of very painful necrosis at the toes
Systolic bruit at the femoral artery

questions.question_text: The following methods are used for confirmation of ischemic form of diabetic foot:

answers.text: Transcutaneous determination of O2 pressure on the foot (ТсpO2)
Determination of toe systolic pressure by photoplethysmography

questions.question_text: Superficial infection of diabetic foot involves:
Deep infection of diabetic foot involves:
According to Wagner classification, localized gangrene of toes is classified as:
According to Wagner classification, foot with extensive gangrene is classified as:
According to Wagner classification, grade 0 diabetic foot means:

answers.text: Superficial full-thickness ulcer (not extending through the subcutis)
Deep ulcer with osteomyelitis / abscess formation

questions.question_text: Choose the drugs which are used for treatment and prevention of vascular complications in patients with diabetic foot syndrome:
Specify the abnormal changes in the laboratory tests, characteristic for diabetic foot infection.
The initial treatment of ischemic form of the diabetic foot Wagner grade IV should include:

answers.text: Skin grafting of the foot ulcer
Primary leg amputation at the level above the ankles
